preamble: While a direct shift from [v] to [l] is rare, the appearance of [l] in positions formerly occupied by or adjacent to [v] is a hallmark of Slavic historical phonology known as Epenthetic L (L-epentheticum). In these cases, an [l] was inserted after labial consonants (including [v]) when followed by the palatal semivowel [j], effectively offloading the palatality onto a new lateral sound.

examples.note: 1st person singular 'I catch'. The 'l' appears to resolve the historical 'v + j' cluster.
Regular correspondence where the Bats labial consonant corresponds to a lateral sound in Chechen.

sources: Townsend, C. E., & Janda, L. A. (1996). Common and Comparative Slavic: Phonology and Inflection.
